title: Video game console

infobox.value: is an [electronic device](/wiki/Electronic_device "Electronic device") that [outputs](/wiki/Input/output "Input/output") a [video signal](/wiki/Video_signal "Video signal") or image to display a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") that can typically be played with a [game controller](/wiki/Game_controller "Game controller"). These may be [home consoles](/wiki/Home_video_game_console "Home video game console"), which are generally placed in a permanent location connected to a television or other display devices and controlled with a separate game controller, or [handheld consoles](/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console"), which include their own display unit and controller functions built into the unit and which can be played anywhere. Hybrid consoles combine elements of both home and handheld consoles.

A **video game console** is an [electronic device](/wiki/Electronic_device "Electronic device") that [outputs](/wiki/Input/output "Input/output") a [video signal](/wiki/Video_signal "Video signal") or image to display a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") that can typically be played with a [game controller](/wiki/Game_controller "Game controller"). These may be [home consoles](/wiki/Home_video_game_console "Home video game console"), which are generally placed in a permanent location connected to a television or other display devices and controlled with a separate game controller, or [handheld consoles](/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console"), which include their own display unit and controller functions built into the unit and which can be played anywhere. Hybrid consoles combine elements of both home and handheld consoles.

Video game consoles are a specialized form of [home computer](/wiki/Home_computer "Home computer") geared towards video game playing, designed with affordability and [accessibility](/wiki/Accessibility "Accessibility") to the general public in mind, but lacking in raw computing power and customization. Simplicity is achieved in part through the use of [game cartridges](/wiki/Game_cartridge "Game cartridge") or other simplified methods of distribution, easing the effort of launching a game. However, this leads to ubiquitous [proprietary formats](/wiki/Proprietary_format "Proprietary format") that create competition for market share.[[1]](#cite_note-1) More recent consoles have shown further confluence with home computers, making it easy for developers to release games on multiple platforms. Further, modern consoles can serve as replacements for [media players](/wiki/Digital_media_player "Digital media player") with capabilities to play films and music from optical media or streaming media services.

Video game consoles are usually sold on a five–seven-year cycle called a generation, with consoles made with similar technical capabilities or made around the same time period grouped into one generation. The industry has developed a [razor and blades model](/wiki/Razor_and_blades_model "Razor and blades model"): manufacturers often sell consoles at low prices, sometimes at a loss, while primarily making a profit from the licensing fees for each game sold. [Planned obsolescence](/wiki/Planned_obsolescence "Planned obsolescence") then draws consumers into buying the next console generation. While numerous manufacturers have come and gone in the history of the console market, there have always been two or three dominant leaders in the market, with the current market led by [Sony](/wiki/Sony_Interactive_Entertainment "Sony Interactive Entertainment") (with their [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation "PlayStation") brand), [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") (with their [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox "Xbox") brand), and [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") (currently producing the [Switch 2](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch_2 "Nintendo Switch 2") and [Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch") consoles). Previous console developers include [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega"), [Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari"), [Coleco](/wiki/Coleco "Coleco"), [Mattel](/wiki/Mattel "Mattel"), [NEC](/wiki/NEC "NEC"), [SNK](/wiki/SNK "SNK"), [Magnavox](/wiki/Magnavox "Magnavox"), [Philips](/wiki/Philips "Philips") and [Panasonic](/wiki/Panasonic "Panasonic").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [History of video game consoles](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles "History of video game consoles") and [Home video game console generations](/wiki/Home_video_game_console_generations "Home video game console generations")

The first video game consoles were produced in the early 1970s. [Ralph H. Baer](/wiki/Ralph_H._Baer "Ralph H. Baer") devised the concept of playing simple, spot-based games on a television screen in 1966, which later became the basis of the [Magnavox Odyssey](/wiki/Magnavox_Odyssey "Magnavox Odyssey") in 1972. Inspired by the table tennis game on the Odyssey, [Nolan Bushnell](/wiki/Nolan_Bushnell "Nolan Bushnell"), [Ted Dabney](/wiki/Ted_Dabney "Ted Dabney"), and [Allan Alcorn](/wiki/Allan_Alcorn "Allan Alcorn") at [Atari, Inc.](/wiki/Atari,_Inc. "Atari, Inc.") developed the first successful [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game"), *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")*, and looked to develop that into a home version, which was released in 1975. The first consoles were capable of playing only a very limited number of games built into the hardware. Programmable consoles using swappable [ROM cartridges](/wiki/ROM_cartridge "ROM cartridge") were introduced with the [Fairchild Channel F](/wiki/Fairchild_Channel_F "Fairchild Channel F") in 1976, though popularized with the [Atari 2600](/wiki/Atari_Video_Computer_System "Atari Video Computer System") released in 1977.

Handheld consoles emerged from technology improvements in [handheld electronic games](/wiki/Handheld_electronic_game "Handheld electronic game") as these shifted from mechanical to electronic/digital logic, and away from [light-emitting diode](/wiki/Light-emitting_diode "Light-emitting diode") (LED) indicators to [liquid-crystal displays](/wiki/Liquid-crystal_display "Liquid-crystal display") (LCD) that resembled video screens more closely. Early examples include the [Microvision](/wiki/Microvision "Microvision") in 1979 and [Game & Watch](/wiki/Game_%26_Watch "Game & Watch") in 1980, and the concept was fully realized by the [Game Boy](/wiki/Game_Boy "Game Boy") in 1989.

Both home and handheld consoles have become more advanced following global changes in technology; These technological shifts include improved electronic and computer chip manufacturing to increase computational power at lower costs and size, the introduction of 3D graphics and hardware-based graphic processors for real-time rendering, digital communications such as the Internet, wireless networking and Bluetooth, and larger and denser media formats as well as digital distribution.

Following the same type of [Moore's law](/wiki/Moore%27s_law "Moore's law") progression, home consoles are grouped into generations; each lasting approximately five years; Consoles within each generation share similar specifications and features, such as [processor word size](/wiki/Word_(computer_architecture) "Word (computer architecture)"). While no one grouping of consoles by generation is universally accepted,[[2]](#cite_note-winner_take_some-2) a breakdown of generations, showing representative consoles of each, is shown below.

### Home video game console

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Home video game console")]

See also: [List of home video game consoles](/wiki/List_of_home_video_game_consoles "List of home video game consoles")

Home video game consoles are meant to be connected to a [television](/wiki/Television_set "Television set") or other type of monitor, with power supplied through an outlet. This requires the unit to be used in a fixed location, typically at home in one's living room. Separate game controllers, connected through wired or wireless connections, are used to provide input to the game. Early examples include the [Atari 2600](/wiki/Atari_2600 "Atari 2600"), the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System"), and the [Sega Genesis](/wiki/Sega_Genesis "Sega Genesis"); newer examples include the [Wii U](/wiki/Wii_U "Wii U"), the [PlayStation 5](/wiki/PlayStation_5 "PlayStation 5"), and the [Xbox Series X](/wiki/Xbox_Series_X "Xbox Series X").

### Microconsole

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Microconsole")]

A [microconsole](/wiki/Microconsole "Microconsole") is a home video game console that is typically powered by low-cost computing hardware, making the console lower-priced compared to other home consoles on the market. The majority of microconsoles, with a few exceptions such as the [PlayStation TV](/wiki/PlayStation_TV "PlayStation TV") and [OnLive Game System](/wiki/OnLive "OnLive"), are [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)")-based digital media players that are bundled with gamepads and marketed as gaming devices. Such microconsoles can be connected to the television to play video games downloaded from an application store such as [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Handheld game console

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Handheld game console")]

See also: [List of handheld game consoles](/wiki/List_of_handheld_game_consoles "List of handheld game consoles")

[Handheld game consoles](/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console") are devices that typically include a built-in screen and game controller in their case, and contain a rechargeable battery or battery compartment. This allows the unit to be carried around and played anywhere, in contrast to a home game console. Examples include the [Game Boy](/wiki/Game_Boy "Game Boy"), the [PlayStation Portable](/wiki/PlayStation_Portable "PlayStation Portable"), and the [Nintendo 3DS](/wiki/Nintendo_3DS "Nintendo 3DS").

### Hybrid video game console

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hybrid video game console")]

Hybrid video game consoles are devices that combine the use as either a handheld or a home console. In addition to handheld consoles; They generally also have either a wired connection or [docking station](/wiki/Docking_station "Docking station") that connects the console unit to a television screen. The consoles can be used as separate controllers only and be played during wired battery charging. Handhelds include the [Sega Nomad](/wiki/Sega_Nomad "Sega Nomad"), [PlayStation Portable](/wiki/PlayStation_Portable "PlayStation Portable"), and Nvidia's [Shield Portable](/wiki/Shield_Portable "Shield Portable") and [Shield Tablet](/wiki/Shield_Tablet "Shield Tablet"), or home consoles such as the [Wii U](/wiki/Wii_U "Wii U") have hybrid features.[[4]](#cite_note-4) With the [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch"), a console with detachable controllers called [Joy-Con](/wiki/Joy-Con "Joy-Con") Set, the hybrid term became popular and is considered by some to be the first truly hybrid console.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Functionality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Functionality")]

Most consoles are considered programmable consoles and have the means for the player to switch between different games. Traditionally, this has been done by switching a physical [game cartridge or game card](/wiki/ROM_cartridge "ROM cartridge") or by using [optical media](/wiki/Optical_media "Optical media"). It is now common to download games through [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution_of_video_games "Digital distribution of video games") and store them on internal or external digital storage devices.

### Dedicated console

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Dedicated console")]

[![Image of the Sega Genesis Mini](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Sega_Genesis_Mini_02.jpg/250px-Sega_Genesis_Mini_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sega_Genesis_Mini_02.jpg)

The [Sega Genesis (Mega Drive) Mini](/wiki/Sega_Genesis_Mini "Sega Genesis Mini") dedicated console

Some consoles are considered [dedicated consoles](/wiki/Dedicated_console "Dedicated console"), in which [games](/wiki/Game "Game") available for the console are "baked" onto the hardware, either by being programmed via the circuitry or set in the read-only flash memory of the console. Thus, the console's game library cannot be added to or changed directly by the user. The user can typically switch between games on dedicated consoles using hardware switches on the console, or through in-game menus. Dedicated consoles were common in the first generation of home consoles, such as the [Magnavox Odyssey](/wiki/Magnavox_Odyssey "Magnavox Odyssey") and the [home console version of *Pong*](/wiki/Pong#Home_version "Pong"), and more recently have been used for retro style consoles such as the [NES Classic Edition](/wiki/NES_Classic_Edition "NES Classic Edition") and [Sega Genesis Mini](/wiki/Sega_Genesis_Mini "Sega Genesis Mini").

Dedicated consoles were very popular in the [first generation](/wiki/First_generation_of_video_game_consoles "First generation of video game consoles") until they were gradually replaced by [second generation](/wiki/Second_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Second generation of video game consoles") that use [ROM cartridges](/wiki/ROM_cartridge "ROM cartridge"). The [fourth generation](/wiki/Fourth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fourth generation of video game consoles") gradually merged with [optical media](/wiki/Optical_media "Optical media").

### Retro style console

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Retro style console")]

See also: [List of retro style video game consoles](/wiki/List_of_retro_style_video_game_consoles "List of retro style video game consoles")

During the later part of video game history, there have been specialized consoles using computing components to offer multiple games to players. Most of these plug directly into one's television, and thus are often called plug-and-play consoles. Most of them are also considered dedicated consoles since it is generally impossible to access the computing components by an average consumer, though tech-savvy consumers often have found ways to [hack](/wiki/Hacker "Hacker") the console to install additional functionality, voiding the manufacturer's warranty. Plug-and-play consoles usually come with the console unit itself, one or more controllers, and the required components for power and video hookup. Many recent plug-and-play releases have been for distributing a number of [retro games](/wiki/Retrogaming "Retrogaming") for a specific console platform. Examples of these include the [Atari Flashback series](/wiki/Atari_Flashback_series "Atari Flashback series"), the [NES Classic Edition](/wiki/NES_Classic_Edition "NES Classic Edition"), [Sega Genesis Mini](/wiki/Sega_Genesis_Mini "Sega Genesis Mini")[[7]](#cite_note-usgamer_plugplay-7) and also handheld retro consoles such as the Nintendo [Game & Watch color screen](/wiki/Game_%26_Watch:_Super_Mario_Bros. "Game & Watch: Super Mario Bros.") series.

## Components

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Components")]

### Console unit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Console unit")]

Early console hardware was designed as customized [printed circuit boards](/wiki/Printed_circuit_board "Printed circuit board") (PCB)s, selecting existing [integrated circuit](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") chips that performed known functions, or programmable chips like [erasable programmable read-only memory](/wiki/EPROM "EPROM") (EPROM) chips that could perform certain functions. Persistent computer memory was expensive, so dedicated consoles were generally limited to the use of [processor registers](/wiki/Processor_register "Processor register") for storage of the state of a game, thus limiting the complexities of such titles.[*[*non sequitur*](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify") (registers are not persistent memory)*] *Pong* in both its arcade and home format, had a handful of logic and calculation chips that used the current input of the players' paddles and registers storing the ball's position to update the game's state and send it to the display device.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Even with more advanced [integrated circuits](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") (IC)s of the time, designers were limited to what could be done through the electrical process rather than through programming as normally associated with [video game development](/wiki/Video_game_development "Video game development").

Improvements in console hardware followed with improvements in microprocessor technology and [semiconductor device fabrication](/wiki/Semiconductor_device_fabrication "Semiconductor device fabrication").[[9]](#cite_note-cpu_improve-9) Manufacturing processes have been able to reduce the feature size on chips (typically measured in [nanometers](/wiki/Nanometers "Nanometers")), allowing more [transistors](/wiki/Transistor "Transistor") and other components to fit on a chip, and at the same time increasing the circuit speeds and the potential [frequency](/wiki/Clock_rate "Clock rate") the chip can run at, as well as reducing [thermal dissipation](/wiki/Thermal_management_(electronics) "Thermal management (electronics)"). Chips were able to be made on larger [dies](/wiki/Die_(integrated_circuit) "Die (integrated circuit)"), further increasing the number of features and effective processing power. [Random-access memory](/wiki/Random-access_memory "Random-access memory") became more practical with the higher density of transistors per chip, but to [address the correct blocks of memory](/wiki/Memory_address "Memory address"), processors needed to be updated to use larger [word sizes](/wiki/Word_(computer_architecture) "Word (computer architecture)") and allot for larger [bandwidth](/wiki/Bandwidth_(computing) "Bandwidth (computing)") in chip communications.[[9]](#cite_note-cpu_improve-9) All these improvements did increase the cost of manufacturing, but at a rate far less than the gains in overall processing power, which helped to make home computers and consoles inexpensive for the consumer, all related to Moore's law of technological improvements.[[9]](#cite_note-cpu_improve-9)

For the consoles of the 1980s to 1990s, these improvements were evident in the marketing in the late 1980s to 1990s during the "bit wars", where console manufacturers had focused on their console's processor's word size as a selling point.[[10]](#cite_note-Therrien-10) Consoles since the 2000s are more similar to personal computers, building in memory, storage features, and networking capabilities to avoid the limitations of the past.[[11]](#cite_note-williams-11) The confluence with personal computers eased software development for both computer and console games, allowing developers to target both platforms. However, consoles differ from computers as most of the hardware components are preselected and customized between the console manufacturer and hardware component provider to assure a consistent performance target for developers. Whereas personal computer motherboards are designed with the needs for allowing consumers to add their desired selection of hardware components, the fixed set of hardware for consoles enables console manufacturers to optimize the size and design of the motherboard and hardware, often integrating key hardware components into the motherboard circuitry itself. Often, multiple components, such as the central processing unit and graphics processing unit, can be combined into a single chip, otherwise known as a [system on a chip](/wiki/System_on_a_chip "System on a chip") (SoC), which is a further reduction in size and cost.[[12]](#cite_note-extremetech_insides-12) In addition, consoles tend to focus on components that give the unit high game performance, such as the CPU and GPU, and as a tradeoff to keep their prices in expected ranges, use less memory and storage space compared to typical personal computers.[[13]](#cite_note-fund_game_dev-13)

In comparison to the early years of the industry, where most consoles were made directly by the company selling the console, many consoles of today are generally constructed through a [value chain](/wiki/Value_chain "Value chain") that includes component suppliers, such as [AMD](/wiki/AMD "AMD") and [NVidia](/wiki/NVidia "NVidia") for CPU and GPU functions, and [contract manufacturers](/wiki/Contract_manufacturer "Contract manufacturer") including [electronics manufacturing services](/wiki/Electronics_manufacturing_services "Electronics manufacturing services"), factories which assemble those components into the final consoles such as [Foxconn](/wiki/Foxconn "Foxconn") and [Flextronics](/wiki/Flextronics "Flextronics"). Completed consoles are then usually tested, distributed, and repaired by the company itself.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Microsoft and Nintendo both use this approach to their consoles, while Sony maintains all production in-house with the exception of their component suppliers.

Some of the commons elements that can be found within console hardware include:

[Motherboard](/wiki/Motherboard "Motherboard")
:   The primary PCB that all of the main chips, including the CPU, are mounted on.

[Daughterboard](/wiki/Daughterboard "Daughterboard")
:   A secondary PCB that connects to the motherboard that would be used for additional functions. These may include components that can be easily replaced later without having to replace the full motherboard.

[Central processing unit](/wiki/Central_processing_unit "Central processing unit") (CPU)
:   The main processing chip on the console that performs most of the computational workload.
:   The consoles' CPU is generally defined by its [word size](/wiki/Word_(computer_architecture) "Word (computer architecture)") (such as [8-bit](/wiki/8-bit_computing "8-bit computing") or [64-bit](/wiki/64-bit_computing "64-bit computing")), and its [clock speed or frequency](/wiki/Clock_rate "Clock rate") in [hertz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz"). For some CPUs, the clock speed can be variable in response to software needs. In general, larger word sizes and faster clock sizes indicate better performance, but other factors will impact the actual speed.
:   Another distinguishing feature for a console's CPU is the [instruction set architecture](/wiki/Instruction_set_architecture "Instruction set architecture"). The instruction set defines low-level [machine code](/wiki/Machine_code "Machine code") to be sent to the CPU to achieve specific results on the chip. Differences in the instruction set architecture of CPU of consoles of a given generation can make for difficulty in [software portability](/wiki/Software_portability "Software portability"). This had been used by manufacturers to keep software titles exclusive to their platform as one means to compete with others.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Consoles prior to the sixth generation typically used chips that the hardware and software developers were most familiar with, but as personal computers stabilized on the [x86](/wiki/X86 "X86") architecture, console manufacturers followed suit as to help easily port games between computer and console.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
:   Newer CPUs may also feature [multiple processing cores](/wiki/Multi-core_processor "Multi-core processor"), which are also identified in their specification. Multi-core CPUs allow for [multithreading](/wiki/Multithreading_(computer_architecture) "Multithreading (computer architecture)") and [parallel computing](/wiki/Parallel_computing "Parallel computing") in modern games, such as one thread for managing the game's rendering engine, one for the game's physics engine, and another for evaluating the player's input.

[Graphical processing unit](/wiki/Graphical_processing_unit "Graphical processing unit") (GPU)
:   The processing unit that performs [rendering](/wiki/Rendering_(computer_graphics) "Rendering (computer graphics)") of data from the CPU to the video output of the console.
:   In the earlier console generations, this was generally limited to simple graphic processing routines, such as [bitmapped graphics](/wiki/Bitmapped_graphics "Bitmapped graphics") and manipulation of [sprites](/wiki/Sprite_(computer_graphics) "Sprite (computer graphics)"), all otherwise involving integer mathematics while minimizing the amount of required memory needed to complete these routines, as memo. For example, the Atari 2600 used its own [Television Interface Adaptor](/wiki/Television_Interface_Adaptor "Television Interface Adaptor") that handled video and audio, while the Nintendo Entertainment System used the [Picture Processing Unit](/wiki/Picture_Processing_Unit "Picture Processing Unit"). For consoles, these GPUs were also designed to send the signal in the proper analog formation to a [cathode ray](/wiki/Cathode_ray "Cathode ray") television, [NTSC](/wiki/NTSC "NTSC") (used in Japan and North America) or [PAL](/wiki/PAL "PAL") (mostly used in Europe). These two formats differed by their [refresh rates](/wiki/Refresh_rate "Refresh rate"), 60 versus 50 [Hertz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz"), and consoles and games that were manufactured for PAL markets used the CPU and GPU at lower frequencies.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
:   The introduction of real-time polygonal 3D graphics rendering in the early 1990s—not just an innovation in video games for consoles but in arcade and personal computer games—led to the development of GPUs that were capable of performing the [floating-point](/wiki/Floating-point "Floating-point") calculations needed for real-time 3D rendering. In contrast to the CPU, modern GPUs for consoles and computers, principally made by [AMD](/wiki/AMD "AMD") and [NVidia](/wiki/NVidia "NVidia"), are highly [parallel computing](/wiki/Parallel_computing "Parallel computing") devices with a number of compute units/streaming multiprocessors (depending on vendor, respectively) within a single chip. Each compute unit/microprocessor contains a [scheduler](/wiki/Scheduling_(computing) "Scheduling (computing)"), a number of subprocessing units, memory caches and buffers, and dispatching and collecting units which also may be highly parallel in nature. Modern console GPUs can be run at a different frequency from the CPU, even at variable frequencies to increases its processing power at the cost of higher energy draw.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The performance of GPUs in consoles can be estimated through [floating-point operations per second](/wiki/FLOPS "FLOPS") (FLOPS) and more commonly as in teraflops (TFLOPS = 1012 FLOPS). However, particularly for consoles, this is considered a rough number as several other factors such as the CPU, memory bandwidth, and console architecture can impact the GPU's true performance.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

[Coprocessors](/wiki/Coprocessors "Coprocessors")
:   Additional processors used to handle other dedicated functions on the console. Many early consoles feature an audio coprocessor for example.

[Northbridge](/wiki/Northbridge_(computing) "Northbridge (computing)")
:   The processor unit that, outside of the CPU and GPU, typically manages the fastest processing elements on the computer. Typically this involves communication of data between the CPU, the GPU, and the on-board RAM, and subsequently sending and receiving information with the southbridge.

[Southbridge](/wiki/Southbridge_(computing) "Southbridge (computing)")
:   The counterpart of the northbridge, the southbridge is the processing unit that handles slower processing components of the console, typically those of [input/output](/wiki/Input/output "Input/output") (I/O) with some internal storage and other connected devices like controllers.

[BIOS](/wiki/BIOS "BIOS")
:   The console's BIOS (Basic Input/Output System) is the fundamental instruction set baked into a firmware chip on the console circuit board that the console uses when it is first turned on to direct operations. In older consoles, prior to the introduction of onboard storage, the BIOS effectively served as the console's operating system, while in modern consoles, the BIOS is used to direct loading of the console's operating system off internal memory.

[Random-access memory](/wiki/Random-access_memory "Random-access memory") (RAM)
:   Memory storage that is designed for fast reading and writing, often used in consoles to store large amounts of data about a game while it is being played to avoid reading from the slower game media. RAM memory typically does not sustain itself after the console is powered off. Besides the amount of RAM available, a key measurement of performance for consoles is the RAM's bandwidth, how fast in terms of bytes per second that the RAM can be written and read from. This is data that must be transferred to and from the CPU and GPU quickly as needed without requiring these chips to need high memory caches themselves.

Internal storage
:   Newer consoles have included internal storage devices, such as [flash memory](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory"), [hard disk drives](/wiki/Hard_disk_drive "Hard disk drive") (HDD) and [solid-state drives](/wiki/Solid-state_drive "Solid-state drive") (SSD), to save data persistently. Early application of internal storage was for saving game states, and more recently can be used to store the console's operating system, game patches and updates, games downloaded through the Internet, additional content for those games, and additional media such as purchased movies and music. Most consoles provide the means to manage the data on this storage while respecting the copyrights on the system. Newer consoles, such as the [PlayStation 5](/wiki/PlayStation_5 "PlayStation 5") and [Xbox Series X](/wiki/Xbox_Series_X "Xbox Series X"), use high-speed SSD's not only for storage but to augment the console's RAM, as the combination of their I/O speeds and the use of decompression routines build into the system software give overall read speeds that approach that of the onboard RAM.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[Power supply](/wiki/Power_supply "Power supply")
:   Besides converting [AC power](/wiki/Alternating_current "Alternating current") from a wall socket to the [DC power](/wiki/Direct_current "Direct current") needed by the console electronics, the power supply also helps to regulate that power in cases of power surges. Some consoles power supplies are built into the unit, so that the consumer plugs the unit directly to a wall socket, but more often, the console ships with an [AC adapter](/wiki/AC_adapter "AC adapter"), colloquially known as a "power brick", that converts the power outside of the unit. On handheld units the power supply will either be from a battery compartment, or optionally from a direct power connection from an AC adapter, or from a rechargeable battery pack built into the unit.

Cooling systems
:   More advanced computing systems generate heat, and require active cooling systems to keep the hardware at safe operating temperatures. Many newer consoles are designed with [cooling fans](/wiki/Computer_fan "Computer fan"), engineered [cooling fins](/wiki/Fin_(extended_surface) "Fin (extended surface)"), internal layouts, and strategically-placed vents on the casing to assure good [convective heat transfer](/wiki/Convection_(heat_transfer) "Convection (heat transfer)") for keeping the internal components cool.

Media reader
:   Since the introduction of game cartridges, nearly all consoles have a cartridge port/reader or an optical drive for game media. In the latter console generations, some console revisions have offered options without a media reader as a means to reduce the console's cost and letting the consumer rely on digital distribution for game acquisition, such as with the [Xbox One S All-Digital Edition](/wiki/Xbox_One_S_All-Digital_Edition "Xbox One S All-Digital Edition") or the [PlayStation 5 Digital Edition](/wiki/PlayStation_5_Digital_Edition "PlayStation 5 Digital Edition").

Case
:   All consoles are enclosed in a case to protect the electronics from damage and to constrain the air flow for cooling.

Input/output ports
:   Ports for connecting power, controllers, televisions or video monitors, external storage devices, Internet connectivity, and other features are placed in strategic locations on the console. Controller connections are typically offered on the front of the console, while power and most other connections are usually found on the back to keep cables out of the way.

### Controllers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Controllers")]

Main article: [Game controller](/wiki/Game_controller "Game controller")

All game consoles require player input through a game controller to provide a method to move the player character in a specific direction and a variation of buttons to perform other in-game actions such as jumping or interacting with the game world.[[21]](#cite_note-McGuireJenkins20093-21) Though controllers have become more featured over the years, they still provide less control over a game compared to personal computers or mobile gaming.[[22]](#cite_note-Fullerton2008-22) The type of controller available to a game can fundamentally change the style of how a console game will or can be played.[[23]](#cite_note-stanford-23)[[24]](#cite_note-McGuireJenkins2009-24)[[25]](#cite_note-McGuireJenkins20092-25) However, this has also inspired changes in game design to create games that accommodate for the comparatively limited controls available on consoles.[[26]](#cite_note-RouseOgden2005-26)

Controllers have come in a variety of styles over the history of consoles. Some common types include:

[Paddle](/wiki/Paddle_(game_controller) "Paddle (game controller)")
:   A unit with a single knob or dial and usually one or two buttons. Turning the knob typically allows one to move an on-screen object along one axis (such as the paddle in a table tennis game), while the buttons can have additional features.

[Joystick](/wiki/Joystick "Joystick")
:   A unit that has a long handle that can pivot freely along multiple directions along with one or more buttons. The unit senses the direction that the joystick is pushed, allowing for simultaneous movement in two directions within a game.

[Gamepad](/wiki/Gamepad "Gamepad")
:   A unit that contains a variety of buttons, triggers, and directional controls – either [D-pads](/wiki/D-pad "D-pad") or [analog sticks](/wiki/Analog_stick "Analog stick") or both. These have become the most common type of controller since the third generation of console hardware, with designs becoming more detailed to provide a larger array of buttons and directional controls for players while maintaining ergonomic features.

Numerous other controller types exist, including those that support [motion controls](/wiki/Motion_control "Motion control"), [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") support on handhelds and some consoles, and specialized controllers for specific types of games, such as [racing wheels](/wiki/Racing_wheel "Racing wheel") for [racing games](/wiki/Racing_video_game "Racing video game"), [light guns](/wiki/Light_gun "Light gun") for [shooting games](/wiki/Shooting_game "Shooting game"), and [musical instrument controllers](/wiki/Rhythm_game_accessories "Rhythm game accessories") for [rhythm games](/wiki/Rhythm_games "Rhythm games"). Some newer consoles also include optional support for a [mouse](/wiki/Computer_mouse "Computer mouse") and [keyboard](/wiki/Computer_keyboard "Computer keyboard") devices. Some older consoles such as 1988 [Sega Genesis](/wiki/Sega_Genesis "Sega Genesis") aka Mega Drive and 1993 [3DO Interactive Multiplayer](/wiki/3DO_Interactive_Multiplayer "3DO Interactive Multiplayer"), supported optional mice, both with special mice made for them, but the 3DO mouse like that console was a flop, and the mouse for the Sega had very limited game support. The Sega also supported the optional [Menacer](/wiki/Menacer "Menacer"), a wireless infrared light gun, and such were at one point popular for games. It also support [BatterUP](/wiki/BatterUP "BatterUP"), a baseball bat-shaped controller.

A controller may be attached through a wired connection onto the console itself, or in some unique cases like the Famicom hardwired to the console, or with a wireless connection. Controllers require power, either provided by the console via the wired connection, or from batteries or a rechargeable battery pack for wireless connections. Controllers are nominally built into a handheld unit, though some newer ones allow for separate wireless controllers to also be used.

### Game media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Game media")]

While the first game consoles were dedicated game systems, with the games programmed into the console's hardware, the [Fairchild Channel F](/wiki/Fairchild_Channel_F "Fairchild Channel F") introduced the ability to store games in a form separate from the console's internal circuitry, thus allowing the consumer to purchase new games to play on the system. Since the Channel F, nearly all game consoles have featured the ability to purchase and swap games through some form, though those forms have changed with improvements in technology.

[ROM cartridge](/wiki/ROM_cartridge "ROM cartridge") or game cartridge
:   The [read-only memory](/wiki/Read-only_memory "Read-only memory") (ROM) cartridge was introduced with the Fairchild Channel F. A ROM cartridge consist of a [printed circuit board](/wiki/Printed_circuit_board "Printed circuit board") (PCB) housed inside of a plastic casing, with a connector allowing the device to interface with the console. The circuit board can contain a wide variety of components, at the minimum, the read-only memory with the software written on it. Later cartridges were able to introduce additional components onto the circuit board like coprocessors, such as Nintendo's [SuperFX](/wiki/SuperFX "SuperFX") chip, to enhance the performance of the console.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Some consoles such as the [Turbografx-16](/wiki/Turbografx-16 "Turbografx-16") used a [smart card](/wiki/Smart_card "Smart card")-like technology to flatten the cartridge to a credit-card-sized system, which helped to reduce production costs, but limited additional features that could be included onto the circuitry.[[28]](#cite_note-28) PCB-based cartridges waned with the introduction of optical media during the fifth generation of consoles. More recently, ROM cartridges have been based on high memory density, low cost [flash memory](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory"), which allows for easier mass production of games. Sony used this approach for the PlayStation Vita,[[29]](#cite_note-29) and Nintendo continues to use ROM cartridges for its 3DS and Switch products.

[Optical media](/wiki/Optical_media "Optical media")
:   Optical media, such as [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM"), [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD"), and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray"), became the primary format for retail distribution with the fifth generation. The CD-ROM format had gained popularity in the 1990s, in the midst of the fourth generation, and as a game media, CD-ROMs were cheaper and faster to produce, offered much more storage space and allowed for the potential of [full-motion video](/wiki/Full-motion_video "Full-motion video").[[30]](#cite_note-30) Several console manufacturers attempted to offer CD-ROM add-ons to fourth generation consoles, but these were nearly as expensive as the consoles themselves and did not fare well. Instead, the CD-ROM format became integrated into consoles of the fifth generation, with the DVD format present across most by the seventh generation and Blu-ray by the eighth. Console manufacturers have also used proprietary disc formats for copy protection as well, such as the [Nintendo optical disc](/wiki/Nintendo_optical_disc "Nintendo optical disc") used on the GameCube, and Sony's [Universal Media Disc](/wiki/Universal_Media_Disc "Universal Media Disc") on the PlayStation Portable.

[Digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution")
:   Since the seventh generation of consoles, most consoles include integrated connectivity to the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") and both internal and external storage for the console, allowing for players to acquire new games without game media. All three of Nintendo, Sony, and Microsoft offer an integrated storefront for consumers to purchase new games and download them to their console, retaining the consumers' purchases across different consoles, and offering sales and incentives at times.

[Cloud gaming](/wiki/Cloud_gaming "Cloud gaming")
:   As Internet access speeds improved throughout the eighth generation of consoles, cloud gaming had gained further attention as a media format. Instead of downloading games, the consumer plays them directly from a cloud gaming service with inputs performed on the local console sent through the Internet to the server with the rendered graphics and audio sent back. [Latency](/wiki/Network_delay "Network delay") in network transmission remains a core limitation for cloud gaming at the present time.

While [magnetic storage](/wiki/Magnetic_storage "Magnetic storage"), such as [tape drives](/wiki/Tape_drive "Tape drive") and [floppy disks](/wiki/Floppy_disk "Floppy disk"), had been popular for software distribution with early personal computers in the 1980s and 1990s, this format did not see much use in console systems. There were some attempts, such as the [Bally Astrocade](/wiki/Bally_Astrocade "Bally Astrocade") and [APF-M1000](/wiki/APF-M1000 "APF-M1000") using tape drives, as well as the [Disk System](/wiki/Family_Computer_Disk_System "Family Computer Disk System") for the Nintendo Famicom,[[31]](#cite_note-31) and the [Nintendo 64DD](/wiki/Nintendo_64DD "Nintendo 64DD") for the Nintendo 64, but these had limited applications, as magnetic media was more fragile and volatile than game cartridges.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### External storage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: External storage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/PSX-Memory-Card.jpg/250px-PSX-Memory-Card.jpg)](/wiki/File:PSX-Memory-Card.jpg)

A PlayStation memory card

In addition to built-in internal storage, newer consoles often give the consumer the ability to use external storage media to save game data, downloaded games, or other media files from the console. Early iterations of external storage were achieved through the use of flash-based [memory cards](/wiki/Memory_card "Memory card"), first used by the [Neo Geo](/wiki/Neo_Geo_(system) "Neo Geo (system)") but popularized with the PlayStation. Nintendo continues to support this approach with extending the storage capabilities of the 3DS and Switch, standardizing on the current [SD card](/wiki/SD_card "SD card") format. As consoles began incorporating the use of [USB](/wiki/USB "USB") ports, support for USB external hard drives was also added, such as with the Xbox 360.

### Online services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Online services")]

Main article: [Online console gaming](/wiki/Online_console_gaming "Online console gaming")

With Internet-enabled consoles, console manufacturers offer both free and paid-subscription services that provide [value-added services](/wiki/Value-added_service "Value-added service") atop the basic functions of the console. Free services generally offer user identity services and access to a digital storefront, while paid services allow players to play online games, interact with other uses through social networking, use cloud saves for supported games, and gain access to free titles on a rotating basis. Examples of such services include the [Xbox network](/wiki/Xbox_network "Xbox network"), [PlayStation Network](/wiki/PlayStation_Network "PlayStation Network"), and [Nintendo Switch Online](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch_Online "Nintendo Switch Online").

### Console add-ons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Console add-ons")]

Certain consoles saw various add-ons or accessories that were designed to attach to the existing console to extend its functionality. The best example of this was through the various CD-ROM add-ons for consoles of the fourth generation such as the TurboGrafx CD, [Atari Jaguar CD](/wiki/Atari_Jaguar_CD "Atari Jaguar CD"), and the [Sega CD](/wiki/Sega_CD "Sega CD"). Other examples of add-ons include the [32X](/wiki/32X "32X") for the [Sega Genesis](/wiki/Sega_Genesis "Sega Genesis") intended to allow owners of the aging console to play newer games but has several technical faults, and the [Game Boy Player](/wiki/Game_Boy_Player "Game Boy Player") for the GameCube to allow it to play Game Boy games.

### Accessories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Accessories")]

Consumers can often purchase a range of accessories for consoles outside of the above categories. These can include:

[Video camera](/wiki/Video_camera "Video camera")
:   While these can be used with Internet-connected consoles like [webcams](/wiki/Webcam "Webcam") for communication with other friends as they would be used on personal computers, video camera applications on consoles are more commonly used in [augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality")/[mixed reality](/wiki/Mixed_reality "Mixed reality") and [motion sensing](/wiki/Motion_sensing "Motion sensing") games. Devices like the [EyeToy](/wiki/EyeToy "EyeToy") for PlayStation consoles and the [Kinect](/wiki/Kinect "Kinect") for Xbox consoles were center-points for a range of games to support these devices on their respective systems.

Standard [Headsets](/wiki/Headset_(audio) "Headset (audio)")
:   Headsets provide a combination of headphones and a microphone for chatting with other players without disturbing others nearby in the same room.

[Virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") headsets
:   Some virtual reality (VR) headsets can operate independently of consoles or use personal computers for their main processing system. As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit), the only direct VR support on consoles is the [PlayStation VR](/wiki/PlayStation_VR "PlayStation VR"), though support for VR on other consoles is planned by the other manufacturers.

[Docking station](/wiki/Docking_station "Docking station")
:   For handheld systems as well as hybrids such as the Nintendo Switch, the docking station makes it easy to insert a handheld to recharge its battery, and if supported, for connecting the handheld to a television screen.

## Game development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Game development")]

The core development process for a console game is very similar to its counterparts and primarily differs in the high level concept due to demographics[[33]](#cite_note-RollingsAdams2003_2-33) and the technical back-end.[[34]](#cite_note-CrawfordCrawford1984-34) Consoles developers will usually make a development kit available to game developers which they can use to test their games on with more ease than a consumer model.

Early console games were commonly created by a single person and could be changed in a short amount of time due to the simplicity of the games at the time.[[35]](#cite_note-RollingsAdams2003-35) As technology has improved, the development time, complexity and cost of console games has increased dramatically,[[36]](#cite_note-Fullerton2008-22-36) to where the size of a team for an eighth generation game can number in the hundreds.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Similarly, the [programming languages](/wiki/Game_programming "Game programming") used in video game development has changed over time with early games being developed primarily in assembly. As time went on developers had more choice on what they could use based on the availability on the console but some languages became more popular than others.[[36]](#cite_note-Fullerton2008-22-36)

In comparison to PC and mobile games, console game developers must consider the limitations of the hardware their game is being developed for, as it is unlikely to have any major changes between the development phase and release. PC and mobile technology progresses quickly and there are many different configurations of their hardware and software. This is beneficial at the start of a console's life cycle, as the technology will be cutting edge, but as the console ages, developers are forced to work with ageing hardware until the next generation of consoles is released. Earlier consoles games could be developed to take advantage of the fixed limitations of the consoles they were developed for, such as the [MegaDrive](/wiki/MegaDrive "MegaDrive")'s capability of fast scrolling influencing design decisions made for *Sonic the Hedgehog*.[[38]](#cite_note-Schell2008-38)

### Console development kits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Console development kits")]

Main article: [Game development kit](/wiki/Game_development_kit "Game development kit")

Console or game development kits are specialized hardware units that typically include the same components as the console and additional chips and components to allow the unit to be connected to a computer or other monitoring device for debugging purposes. A console manufacturer will make the console's dev kit available to registered developers months ahead of the console's planned launch to give developers time to prepare their games for the new system. These initial kits will usually be offered under special confidentiality clauses to protect trade secrets of the console's design, and will be sold at a high cost to the developer as part of keeping this confidentiality.[[13]](#cite_note-fund_game_dev-13) Newer consoles that share features in common with personal computers may no longer use specialized dev kits, though developers are still expected to register and purchase access to [software development kits](/wiki/Software_development_kit "Software development kit") from the manufacturer. For example, any consumer [Xbox One](/wiki/Xbox_One "Xbox One") can be used for game development after paying a fee to Microsoft to register one intent to do so.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Licensing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Licensing")]

Since the release of the Nintendo Famicom / Nintendo Entertainment System, most video game console manufacturers employ a strict licensing scheme that limit what games can be developed for it. Developers and their publishers must pay a fee, typically based on [royalty](/wiki/Royalty_payment "Royalty payment") per unit sold, back to the manufacturer. The cost varies by manufacturer but was estimated to be about US$3−10 per unit in 2012. With additional fees, such as branding rights, this has generally worked out to be an industry-wide 30% royalty rate paid to the console manufacturer for every game sold.[[40]](#cite_note-ign_licensing-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) This is in addition to the cost of acquiring the dev kit to develop for the system.

The licensing fee may be collected in a few different ways. In the case of Nintendo, the company generally has controlled the production of game cartridges with its lockout chips and optical media for its systems, and thus charges the developer or publisher for each copy it makes as an upfront fee. This also allows Nintendo to review the game's content prior to release and veto games it does not believe appropriate to include on its system. This had led to over 700 unlicensed games for the NES,[[42]](#cite_note-42) and numerous others on other Nintendo cartridge-based systems that had found ways to bypass the hardware lockout chips and sell without paying any royalties to Nintendo, such as by Atari in its subsidiary company [Tengen](/wiki/Tengen_(company) "Tengen (company)").[[43]](#cite_note-43) This licensing approach was similarly used by most other cartridge-based console manufacturers using lockout chip technology.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

With optical media, where the console manufacturer may not have direct control on the production of the media, the developer or publisher typically must establish a licensing agreement to gain access to the console's proprietary storage format for the media as well as to use the console and manufacturer's logos and branding for the game's packaging, paid back through royalties on sales.[[40]](#cite_note-ign_licensing-40) In the transition to digital distribution, where now the console manufacturer runs digital storefronts for games, license fees apply to registering a game for distribution on the storefront – again gaining access to the console's branding and logo – with the manufacturer taking its cut of each sale as its royalty.[[40]](#cite_note-ign_licensing-40) In both cases, this still gives console manufacturers the ability to review and reject games it believes unsuitable for the system and deny licensing rights.

With the rise of [indie game development](/wiki/Indie_game_development "Indie game development"), the major console manufacturers have all developed entry level routes for these smaller developers to be able to publish onto consoles at far lower costs and reduced royalty rates. Programs like Microsoft's [ID@Xbox](/wiki/ID@Xbox "ID@Xbox") give developers most of the needed tools for free after validating the small development size and needs of the team.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Similar licensing concepts apply for third-party accessory manufacturers.[[40]](#cite_note-ign_licensing-40)

## Emulation and backward compatibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Emulation and backward compatibility")]

Main articles: [Video game console emulator](/wiki/Video_game_console_emulator "Video game console emulator") and [Backward compatibility](/wiki/Backward_compatibility "Backward compatibility")

Consoles, like most consumer electronic devices, have limited lifespans. There is great interest in [preservation](/wiki/Video_game_preservation "Video game preservation") of older console hardware for archival and historical purposes, as games from older consoles, as well as arcade and personal computers, remain of interest. Computer programmers and hackers have developed emulators that can be run on personal computers or other consoles that simulate the hardware of older consoles that allow games from that console to be run. The development of software emulators of console hardware is established to be legal, but there are unanswered legal questions surrounding [copyrights](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright"), including acquiring a console's [firmware](/wiki/Firmware "Firmware") and copies of a game's [ROM image](/wiki/ROM_image "ROM image"), which laws such as the United States' [Digital Millennium Copyright Act](/wiki/Digital_Millennium_Copyright_Act "Digital Millennium Copyright Act") make illegal save for certain archival purposes.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Even though emulation itself is legal, Nintendo is recognized to be highly protective of any attempts to emulate its systems and has taken early legal actions to shut down such projects.[[47]](#cite_note-game_over_white_paper-47)

To help support older games and console transitions, manufacturers started to support backward compatibility on consoles in the same family. Sony was the first to do this on a home console with the [PlayStation 2](/wiki/PlayStation_2 "PlayStation 2") which was able to play original [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)") content, and subsequently became a sought-after feature across many consoles that followed.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Backward compatibility functionality has included direct support for previous console games on the newer consoles such as within the [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox "Xbox") console family,[[49]](#cite_note-49) the distribution of emulated games such as [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo")'s [Virtual Console](/wiki/Virtual_Console "Virtual Console"), or using [cloud gaming](/wiki/Cloud_gaming "Cloud gaming") services for these older games as with the [PlayStation Now](/wiki/PlayStation_Now "PlayStation Now") service.

## Market

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Market")]

### Distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Distribution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/BEST-SELLING_GAME_CONSOLES_VIS2.svg/250px-BEST-SELLING_GAME_CONSOLES_VIS2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:BEST-SELLING_GAME_CONSOLES_VIS2.svg)

Visualization of the Best-Selling game consoles from 1977 to 2024

Consoles may be shipped in a variety of configurations, but typically will include one base configuration that include the console, one controller, and sometimes a [pack-in game](/wiki/Pack-in_game "Pack-in game"). Manufacturers may offer alternate [stock keeping unit](/wiki/Stock_keeping_unit "Stock keeping unit") (SKUs) options that include additional controllers and accessories or different pack-in games. Special console editions may feature unique cases or faceplates with art dedicated to a specific video game or series and are bundled with that game as a special incentive for its fans. Pack-in games are typically first-party games, often featuring the console's primary [mascot characters](/wiki/Video_game_mascot "Video game mascot").[[50]](#cite_note-geemu-50)

The more recent console generations have also seen multiple versions of the same base console system either offered at launch or presented as a mid-generation refresh. In some cases, these simply replace some parts of the hardware with cheaper or more efficient parts, or otherwise streamline the console's design for production going forward; the [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3_technical_specifications "PlayStation 3 technical specifications") underwent several such hardware refreshes during its lifetime due to technological improvements such as significant reduction of the [process node](/wiki/Semiconductor_device_fabrication "Semiconductor device fabrication") size for the CPU and GPU.[[51]](#cite_note-51) In these cases, the hardware revision model will be marked on packaging so that consumers can verify which version they are acquiring.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In other cases, the hardware changes create multiple lines within the same console family. The base console unit in all revisions share fundamental hardware, but options like internal storage space and RAM size may be different. Those systems with more storage and RAM would be marked as a higher performance variant available at a higher cost, while the original unit would remain as a budget option. For example, within the [Xbox One](/wiki/Xbox_One "Xbox One") family, Microsoft released the mid-generation [Xbox One X](/wiki/Xbox_One_X "Xbox One X") as a higher performance console, the [Xbox One S](/wiki/Xbox_One_S "Xbox One S") as the lower-cost base console, and a special [Xbox One S All-Digital Edition](/wiki/Xbox_One_S_All-Digital_Edition "Xbox One S All-Digital Edition") revision that removed the optical drive on the basis that users could download all games digitally, offered at even a lower cost than the Xbox One S. In these cases, developers can often optimize games to work better on the higher-performance console with patches to the retail version of the game.[[53]](#cite_note-53) In the case of the [Nintendo 3DS](/wiki/Nintendo_3DS "Nintendo 3DS"), the [New Nintendo 3DS](/wiki/New_Nintendo_3DS "New Nintendo 3DS"), featured upgraded memory and processors, with new games that could only be run on the upgraded units and cannot be run on an older base unit.[[54]](#cite_note-New_N3DS_at_Polygon-54) There have also been a number of "slimmed-down" console options with significantly reduced hardware components that significantly reduced the price they could sell the console to the consumer, but either leaving certain features off the console, such as the [Wii Mini](/wiki/Wii_Mini "Wii Mini") that lacked any online components compared to the [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii"), or that required the consumer to purchase additional accessories and wiring if they did not already own it, such as the [New-Style NES](/wiki/New-Style_NES "New-Style NES") that was not bundled with the required RF hardware to connect to a television.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Consoles when originally launched in the 1970s and 1980s were about US$200−300,[[56]](#cite_note-arstech_console_price-56) and with the introduction of the ROM cartridge, each game averaged about US$30−40.[[63]](#cite_note-arstech_prices-67) Over time the launch price of base consoles units has generally risen to about US$400−500,[[56]](#cite_note-arstech_console_price-56) with the average game costing US$60.[[63]](#cite_note-arstech_prices-67) Exceptionally, the period of transition from ROM cartridges to optical media in the early 1990s saw several consoles with high price points exceeding US$400 and going as high as US$700. Resultingly, sales of these first optical media consoles were generally poor.[[56]](#cite_note-arstech_console_price-56)

When adjusted for inflation, the price of consoles has generally followed a downward trend, from US$800−1,000 from the early generations down to US$500−600 for current consoles. This is typical for any computer technology, with the improvements in computing performance and capabilities outpacing the additional costs to achieve those gains.[[56]](#cite_note-arstech_console_price-56) Further, within the United States, the price of consoles has generally remained consistent, being within 0.8% to 1% of the median household income, based on the [United States Census](/wiki/United_States_Census "United States Census") data for the console's launch year.[[56]](#cite_note-arstech_console_price-56)

Since the Nintendo Entertainment System, console pricing has stabilized on the [razorblade model](/wiki/Razor_and_blades_model "Razor and blades model"), where the consoles are sold at little to no profit for the manufacturer, but they gain revenue from each game sold due to console licensing fees and other value-added services around the console (such as [Xbox Live](/wiki/Xbox_Live "Xbox Live")).[[47]](#cite_note-game_over_white_paper-47)[[64]](#cite_note-down_many_times-68)[[65]](#cite_note-69) Console manufacturers have even been known to take losses on the sale of consoles at the start of a console's launch with expectation to recover with revenue sharing and later price recovery on the console as they switch to less expensive components and manufacturing processes without changing the retail price.[[66]](#cite_note-70) Consoles have been generally designed to have a five-year [product lifetime](/wiki/Product_lifetime "Product lifetime"), though manufacturers have considered their entries in the more recent generations to have longer lifetimes of seven to potentially ten years.[[67]](#cite_note-71)

### Competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_console&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Competition")]

See also: [Console war](/wiki/Console_war "Console war"), [Video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry"), and [History of video games](/wiki/History_of_video_games "History of video games")

The competition within the video game console market as subset of the video game industry is an area of interest to economics with its relatively modern history, its rapid growth to rival that of the film industry, and frequent changes compared to other sectors.[[57]](#cite_note-gamble_chapter-57)[[11]](#cite_note-williams-11)

Effects of unregulated competition on the market were twice seen early in the industry. The industry had its first crash in 1977 following the release of the Magnavox Odyssey, Atari's home versions of *Pong* and [the Coleco Telstar](/wiki/Coleco_Telstar_series "Coleco Telstar series"), which led other third-party manufacturers, using inexpensive [General Instruments](/wiki/General_Instrument "General Instrument") processor chips, to make their own home consoles which flooded the market by 1977.[[68]](#cite_note-before_crash-72): 81–89 The [video game crash of 1983](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1983 "Video game crash of 1983") was fueled by multiple factors including competition from lower-cost personal computers, but unregulated competition was also a factor, as numerous third-party game developers, attempting to follow on the success of [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision") in developing third-party games for the Atari 2600 and Intellivision, flooded the market with poor quality games, and made it difficult for even quality games to sell.[[69]](#cite_note-prince198309-73) Nintendo implemented a lockout chip, the [Checking Integrated Circuit](/wiki/CIC_(Nintendo) "CIC (Nintendo)"), on releasing the Nintendo Entertainment System in Western territories, as a means to control which games were published for the console. As part of their licensing agreements, Nintendo further prevented developers from releasing the same game on a different console for a period of two years. This served as one of the first means of securing [console exclusivity](/wiki/Platform_exclusivity "Platform exclusivity") for games that existed beyond technical limitation of console development.[[70]](#cite_note-74)

The Nintendo Entertainment System also brought the concept of a [video game mascot](/wiki/List_of_video_game_mascots "List of video game mascots") as the representation of a console system as a means to sell and promote the unit, and for the NES was [Mario](/wiki/Mario "Mario"). The use of mascots in businesses had been a tradition in Japan, and this had already proven successful in arcade games like *[Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man")*. Mario was used to serve as an identity for the NES as a humor-filled, playful console.[[50]](#cite_note-geemu-50)[[71]](#cite_note-kline_nintendo-75) Mario caught on quickly when the NES released in the West, and when the next generation of consoles arrived, other manufacturers pushed their own mascots to the forefront of their marketing, most notably [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") with the use of [Sonic the Hedgehog](/wiki/Sonic_the_Hedgehog_(character) "Sonic the Hedgehog (character)").[[72]](#cite_note-kline_console_wars-76) The Nintendo and Sega rivalry that involved their mascot's flagship games served as part of the fourth console generation's "console wars". Since then, manufacturers have typically positioned their mascot and other first-party games as key titles in console bundles used to drive sales of consoles at launch or at key sales periods such as near Christmas.[[50]](#cite_note-geemu-50)

Another type of competitive edge used by console manufacturers around the same time was the notion of "bits" or the size of the [word](/wiki/Word_(computer_architecture) "Word (computer architecture)") used by the main CPU. The TurboGrafx-16 was the first console to push on its bit-size, advertising itself as a "16-bit" console, though this only referred to part of its architecture while its CPU was still an 8-bit unit. Despite this, manufacturers found consumers became fixated on the notion of bits as a console selling point, and over the fourth, fifth and sixth generation, these "bit wars" played heavily into console advertising.[[10]](#cite_note-Therrien-10) The use of bits waned as CPU architectures no longer needed to increase their word size and instead had other means to improve performance such as through multicore CPUs.[[10]](#cite_note-Therrien-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Sega_Dreamcast_arcade_machine.jpg/250px-Sega_Dreamcast_arcade_machine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sega_Dreamcast_arcade_machine.jpg)

Retail demo kiosk for a [Dreamcast](/wiki/Dreamcast "Dreamcast"), the last console from Sega, at the [Finnish Museum of Games](/wiki/Finnish_Museum_of_Games "Finnish Museum of Games") in [Tampere](/wiki/Tampere "Tampere"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") in 2017

Generally, increased console numbers gives rise to more consumer options and better competition, but the exclusivity of titles made the choice of console for consumers an "all-or-nothing" decision for most.[[11]](#cite_note-williams-11) Further, with the number of available consoles growing with the fifth and sixth generations, game developers became pressured to which systems to focus on, and ultimately narrowed their target choice of platforms to those that were the best-selling. This cased a contraction in the market, with major players like Sega leaving the hardware business after the Dreamcast but continuing in the software area.[[57]](#cite_note-gamble_chapter-57) Effectively, each console generation was shown to have two or three dominant players.[[11]](#cite_note-williams-11)

Competition in the console market in the 2010s and 2020s is considered an [oligopoly](/wiki/Oligopoly "Oligopoly") between three main manufacturers: Nintendo, Sony, and Microsoft. The three use a combination of first-party games exclusive to their console and negotiate exclusive agreements with third-party developers to have their games be exclusive for at least an initial period of time to drive consumers to their console. They also worked with CPU and GPU manufacturers to tune and customize hardware for computers to make it more amenable and effective for video games, leading to lower-cost hardware needed for video game consoles. Finally, console manufacturers also work with retailers to help with promotion of consoles, games, and accessories. While there is little difference in pricing on the console hardware from the [manufacturer's suggested retail price](/wiki/Manufacturer%27s_suggested_retail_price "Manufacturer's suggested retail price") for the retailer to profit from, these details with the manufacturers can secure better profits on sales of game and accessory bundles for premier product placement.[[57]](#cite_note-gamble_chapter-57) These all form [network effects](/wiki/Network_effect "Network effect"), with each manufacturer seeking to maximize the size of their network of partners to increase their overall position in the competition.[[11]](#cite_note-williams-11)

Of the three, Microsoft and Sony, both with their own hardware manufacturing capabilities, remain at a leading edge approach, attempting to gain a [first-mover advantage](/wiki/First-mover_advantage "First-mover advantage") over the other with adaption of new console technology.[[57]](#cite_note-gamble_chapter-57) Nintendo is more reliant on its suppliers and thus instead of trying to compete feature for feature with Microsoft and Sony, had instead taken a ["blue ocean" strategy](/wiki/Blue_Ocean_Strategy "Blue Ocean Strategy") since the [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS") and [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii").[[73]](#cite_note-77)
